        and `is2` *bytes*, respectively. This function requires
        behaved (though not necessarily contiguous) memory.
    int scanfunc(FILE \*fd, void \*ip, void \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.scanfunc "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to a function that scans (scanf style) one element
        of the corresponding type from the file descriptor `fd` into
        the array memory pointed to by `ip`. The array is assumed
        to be behaved.
        The last argument `arr` is the array to be scanned into.
        Returns number of receiving arguments successfully assigned (which
        may be zero in case a matching failure occurred before the first
        receiving argument was assigned), or EOF if input failure occurs
        before the first receiving argument was assigned.
        This function should be called without holding the Python GIL, and
        has to grab it for error reporting.
    int fromstr(char \*str, void \*ip, char \*\*endptr, void \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.fromstr "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to a function that converts the string pointed to by
        `str` to one element of the corresponding type and places it
        in the memory location pointed to by `ip`. After the
        conversion is completed, `*endptr` points to the rest of the
        string. The last argument `arr` is the array into which ip
        points (needed for variable-size data- types). Returns 0 on
        success or -1 on failure. Requires a behaved array.
        This function should be called without holding the Python GIL, and
        has to grab it for error reporting.
    [npy\_bool](dtype.html#c.npy_bool "npy_bool") nonzero(void \*data, void \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.nonzero "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to a function that returns TRUE if the item of
        `arr` pointed to by `data` is nonzero. This function can
        deal with misbehaved arrays.
    void fill(void \*data, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") length, void \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.fill "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to a function that fills a contiguous array of given
        length with data. The first two elements of the array must
        already be filled- in. From these two values, a delta will be
        computed and the values from item 3 to the end will be
        computed by repeatedly adding this computed delta. The data
        buffer must be well-behaved.
    void fillwithscalar(void \*buffer, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") length, void \*value, void \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.fillwithscalar "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to a function that fills a contiguous `buffer` of
        the given `length` with a single scalar `value` whose
        address is given. The final argument is the array which is
        needed to get the itemsize for variable-length arrays.
    int sort(void \*start, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") length, void \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.sort "Link to this definition")
    :   An array of function pointers to a particular sorting
        algorithms. A particular sorting algorithm is obtained using a
        key (so far [`NPY_QUICKSORT`](array.html#c.NPY_SORTKIND.NPY_QUICKSORT "NPY_SORTKIND.NPY_QUICKSORT"), [`NPY_HEAPSORT`](array.html#c.NPY_SORTKIND.NPY_HEAPSORT "NPY_SORTKIND.NPY_HEAPSORT"),
        and [`NPY_MERGESORT`](array.html#c.NPY_SORTKIND.NPY_MERGESORT "NPY_SORTKIND.NPY_MERGESORT") are defined). These sorts are done
        in-place assuming contiguous and aligned data.
    int argsort(void \*start, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*result, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") length, void \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.argsort "Link to this definition")
    :   An array of function pointers to sorting algorithms for this
        data type. The same sorting algorithms as for sort are
        available. The indices producing the sort are returned in
        `result` (which must be initialized with indices 0 to
        `length-1` inclusive).
    [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*castdict[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.castdict "Link to this definition")
    :   Either `NULL` or a dictionary containing low-level casting
        functions for user- defined data-types. Each function is
        wrapped in a [PyCapsule](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/capsule.html#c.PyCapsule "(in Python v3.14)")\* and keyed by
        the data-type number.
    [NPY\_SCALARKIND](array.html#c.NPY_SCALARKIND "NPY_SCALARKIND") scalarkind([PyArrayObject](#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.scalarkind "Link to this definition")
    :   A function to determine how scalars of this type should be
        interpreted. The argument is `NULL` or a 0-dimensional array
        containing the data (if that is needed to determine the kind
        of scalar). The return value must be of type
        [`NPY_SCALARKIND`](array.html#c.NPY_SCALARKIND "NPY_SCALARKIND").
    int \*\*cancastscalarkindto[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.cancastscalarkindto "Link to this definition")
    :   Either `NULL` or an array of [`NPY_NSCALARKINDS`](array.html#c.NPY_SCALARKIND.NPY_NSCALARKINDS "NPY_SCALARKIND.NPY_NSCALARKINDS")
        pointers. These pointers should each be either `NULL` or a
        pointer to an array of integers (terminated by
        [`NPY_NOTYPE`](dtype.html#c.NPY_NOTYPE "NPY_NOTYPE")) indicating data-types that a scalar of
        this data-type of the specified kind can be cast to safely
        (this usually means without losing precision).
    int \*cancastto[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.cancastto "Link to this definition")
    :   Either `NULL` or an array of integers (terminated by
        [`NPY_NOTYPE`](dtype.html#c.NPY_NOTYPE "NPY_NOTYPE") ) indicated data-types that this data-type
        can be cast to safely (this usually means without losing
        precision).
    int argmin(void \*data, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") n, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*min\_ind, void \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ArrFuncs.argmin "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to a function that retrieves the index of the
        smallest of `n` elements in `arr` beginning at the element
        pointed to by `data`. This function requires that the
        memory segment be contiguous and behaved. The return value is
        always 0. The index of the smallest element is returned in
        `min_ind`.
### PyArrayMethod\_Context and PyArrayMethod\_Spec[#](#pyarraymethod-context-and-pyarraymethod-spec "Link to this heading")
type PyArrayMethodObject\_tag[#](#c.PyArrayMethodObject_tag "Link to this definition")
:   An opaque struct used to represent the method “self” in ArrayMethod loops.
type PyArrayMethod\_Context[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Context "Link to this definition")
:   A struct that is passed in to ArrayMethod loops to provide context for the
    runtime usage of the loop.
    ```
    typedef struct {
        PyObject *caller;
        struct PyArrayMethodObject_tag *method;
        PyArray_Descr *const *descriptors;
        void *parameters;
    } PyArrayMethod_Context
    ```
    [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*caller[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Context.caller "Link to this definition")
    :   The caller, which is typically the ufunc that called the loop. May be
        `NULL` when a call is not from a ufunc (e.g. casts).
    struct [PyArrayMethodObject\_tag](#c.PyArrayMethodObject_tag "PyArrayMethodObject_tag") \*method[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Context.method "Link to this definition")
    :   The method “self”. Currently this object is an opaque pointer.
    [PyArray\_Descr](#c.PyArray_Descr "PyArray_Descr") \*\*descriptors[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Context.descriptors "Link to this definition")
    :   An array of descriptors for the ufunc loop, filled in by
        `resolve_descriptors`. The length of the array is `nin` + `nout`.
    void \*parameters[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Context.parameters "Link to this definition")
    :   > A pointer to a structure containing any runtime parameters needed by the
        > loop. This is `NULL` if no parameters are needed. The type of the
        > struct is specific to the registered function.
        Changed in version NumPy: 2.4
        The *parameters* member was added in NumPy 2.4.
type PyArrayMethod\_Spec[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec "Link to this definition")
:   A struct used to register an ArrayMethod with NumPy. We use the slots
    mechanism used by the Python limited API. See below for the slot definitions.
    ```
    typedef struct {
       const char *name;
       int nin, nout;
       NPY_CASTING casting;
       NPY_ARRAYMETHOD_FLAGS flags;
       PyArray_DTypeMeta **dtypes;
       PyType_Slot *slots;
    } PyArrayMethod_Spec;
    ```
    const char \*name[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec.name "Link to this definition")
    :   The name of the loop.
    int nin[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec.nin "Link to this definition")
    :   The number of input operands
    int nout[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec.nout "Link to this definition")
    :   The number of output operands.
    [NPY\_CASTING](array.html#c.NPY_CASTING "NPY_CASTING") casting[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec.casting "Link to this definition")
    :   Used to indicate how minimally permissive a casting operation should
        be. For example, if a cast operation might in some circumstances be safe,
        but in others unsafe, then `NPY_UNSAFE_CASTING` should be set. Not used
        for ufunc loops but must still be set.
    [NPY\_ARRAYMETHOD\_FLAGS](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAYMETHOD_FLAGS "NPY_ARRAYMETHOD_FLAGS") flags[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec.flags "Link to this definition")
    :   The flags set for the method.
    [PyArray\_DTypeMeta](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta "PyArray_DTypeMeta") \*\*dtypes[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec.dtypes "Link to this definition")
    :   The DTypes for the loop. Must be `nin` + `nout` in length.
    [PyType\_Slot](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyType_Slot "(in Python v3.14)") \*slots[#](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec.slots "Link to this definition")
    :   An array of slots for the method. Slot IDs must be one of the values
        below.
### PyArray\_DTypeMeta and PyArrayDTypeMeta\_Spec[#](#pyarray-dtypemeta-and-pyarraydtypemeta-spec "Link to this heading")
[PyTypeObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyTypeObject "(in Python v3.14)") PyArrayDTypeMeta\_Type[#](#c.PyArrayDTypeMeta_Type "Link to this definition")
:   The python type object corresponding to [`PyArray_DTypeMeta`](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta "PyArray_DTypeMeta").
type PyArray\_DTypeMeta[#](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta "Link to this definition")
:   A largely opaque struct representing DType classes. Each instance defines a
    metaclass for a single NumPy data type. Data types can either be
    non-parametric or parametric. For non-parametric types, the DType class has
    a one-to-one correspondence with the descriptor instance created from the
    DType class. Parametric types can correspond to many different dtype
    instances depending on the chosen parameters. This type is available in the
    public `numpy/dtype_api.h` header. Currently use of this struct is not
    supported in the limited CPython API, so if `Py_LIMITED_API` is set, this
    type is a typedef for `PyTypeObject`.
    ```
    typedef struct {
         PyHeapTypeObject super;
         PyArray_Descr *singleton;
         int type_num;
         PyTypeObject *scalar_type;
         npy_uint64 flags;
         void *dt_slots;
         void *reserved[3];
    } PyArray_DTypeMeta
    ```
    PyHeapTypeObject super[#](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta.super "Link to this definition")
    :   The superclass, providing hooks into the python object
        API. Set members of this struct to fill in the functions
        implementing the `PyTypeObject` API (e.g. `tp_new`).
    [PyArray\_Descr](#c.PyArray_Descr "PyArray_Descr") \*singleton[#](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta.singleton "Link to this definition")
    :   A descriptor instance suitable for use as a singleton
        descriptor for the data type. This is useful for
        non-parametric types representing simple plain old data type
        where there is only one logical descriptor instance for all
        data of the type. Can be NULL if a singleton instance is not
        appropriate.
    int type\_num[#](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta.type_num "Link to this definition")
    :   Corresponds to the type number for legacy data types. Data
        types defined outside of NumPy and possibly future data types
        shipped with NumPy will have `type_num` set to -1, so this
        should not be relied on to discriminate between data types.
    [PyTypeObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyTypeObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*scalar\_type[#](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta.scalar_type "Link to this definition")
    :   The type of scalar instances for this data type.
    [npy\_uint64](dtype.html#c.npy_uint64 "npy_uint64") flags[#](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta.flags "Link to this definition")
    :   Flags can be set to indicate to NumPy that this data type
        has optional behavior. See [Flags](array.html#dtype-flags) for a listing of
        allowed flag values.
    void \*dt\_slots[#](#c.PyArray_DTypeMeta.dt_slots "Link to this definition")
    :   An opaque pointer to a private struct containing
        implementations of functions in the DType API. This is filled
        in from the `slots` member of the `PyArrayDTypeMeta_Spec`
        instance used to initialize the DType.
type PyArrayDTypeMeta\_Spec[#](#c.PyArrayDTypeMeta_Spec "Link to this definition")
:   A struct used to initialize a new DType with the
    `PyArrayInitDTypeMeta_FromSpec` function.
    ```
    typedef struct {
        PyTypeObject *typeobj;
        int flags;
        PyArrayMethod_Spec **casts;
        PyType_Slot *slots;
        PyTypeObject *baseclass;
    }
    ```
    [PyTypeObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyTypeObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*typeobj[#](#c.PyArrayDTypeMeta_Spec.typeobj "Link to this definition")
    :   Either `NULL` or the type of the python scalar associated with
        the DType. Scalar indexing into an array returns an item with this
        type.
    int flags[#](#c.PyArrayDTypeMeta_Spec.flags "Link to this definition")
    :   Static flags for the DType class, indicating whether the DType is
        parametric, abstract, or represents numeric data. The latter is
        optional but is useful to set to indicate to downstream code if
        the DType represents data that are numbers (ints, floats, or other
        numeric data type) or something else (e.g. a string, unit, or
        date).
    [PyArrayMethod\_Spec](#c.PyArrayMethod_Spec "PyArrayMethod_Spec") \*\*casts;[#](#c.PyArrayDTypeMeta_Spec.casts "Link to this definition")
    :   A `NULL`-terminated array of ArrayMethod specifications for
        casts defined by the DType.
    [PyType\_Slot](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyType_Slot "(in Python v3.14)") \*slots;[#](#c.PyArrayDTypeMeta_Spec.slots "Link to this definition")
    :   A `NULL`-terminated array of slot specifications for implementations
        of functions in the DType API. Slot IDs must be one of the
        DType slot IDs enumerated in [Slot IDs and API Function Typedefs](array.html#dtype-slots).
### Exposed DTypes classes (`PyArray_DTypeMeta` objects)[#](#exposed-dtypes-classes-pyarray-dtypemeta-objects "Link to this heading")
For use with promoters, NumPy exposes a number of Dtypes following the
pattern `PyArray_<Name>DType` corresponding to those found in *np.dtypes*.
Additionally, the three DTypes, `PyArray_PyLongDType`,
`PyArray_PyFloatDType`, `PyArray_PyComplexDType` correspond to the
Python scalar values. These cannot be used in all places, but do allow
for example the common dtype operation and implementing promotion with them
may be necessary.
Further, the following abstract DTypes are defined which cover both the
builtin NumPy ones and the python ones, and users can in principle subclass
from them (this does not inherit any DType specific functionality):
\* `PyArray_IntAbstractDType`
\* `PyArray_FloatAbstractDType`
\* `PyArray_ComplexAbstractDType`
Warning
As of NumPy 2.0, the *only* valid use for these DTypes is registering a
promoter conveniently to e.g. match “any integers” (and subclass checks).
Because of this, they are not exposed to Python.
### PyUFunc\_Type and PyUFuncObject[#](#pyufunc-type-and-pyufuncobject "Link to this heading")
[PyTypeObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyTypeObject "(in Python v3.14)") PyUFunc\_Type[#](#c.PyUFunc_Type "Link to this definition")
:   The ufunc object is implemented by creation of the
    [`PyUFunc_Type`](#c.PyUFunc_Type "PyUFunc_Type"). It is a very simple type that implements only
    basic getattribute behavior, printing behavior, and has call
    behavior which allows these objects to act like functions. The
    basic idea behind the ufunc is to hold a reference to fast
    1-dimensional (vector) loops for each data type that supports the
    operation. These one-dimensional loops all have the same signature
    and are the key to creating a new ufunc. They are called by the
    generic looping code as appropriate to implement the N-dimensional
    function. There are also some generic 1-d loops defined for
    floating and complexfloating arrays that allow you to define a
    ufunc using a single scalar function (*e.g.* atanh).
type PyUFuncObject[#](#c.PyUFuncObject "Link to this definition")
:   The core of the ufunc is the [`PyUFuncObject`](#c.PyUFuncObject "PyUFuncObject") which contains all
    the information needed to call the underlying C-code loops that
    perform the actual work. While it is described here for completeness, it
    should be considered internal to NumPy and manipulated via `PyUFunc_*`
    functions. The size of this structure is subject to change across versions
    of NumPy. To ensure compatibility:
    * Never declare a non-pointer instance of the struct
    * Never perform pointer arithmetic
    * Never use `sizeof(PyUFuncObject)`
    It has the following structure:
    ```
    typedef struct {
        PyObject_HEAD
        int nin;
        int nout;
        int nargs;
        int identity;
        PyUFuncGenericFunction *functions;
        void **data;
        int ntypes;
        int reserved1;
        const char *name;
        char *types;
        const char *doc;
        void *ptr;
        PyObject *obj;
        PyObject *userloops;
        int core_enabled;
        int core_num_dim_ix;
        int *core_num_dims;
        int *core_dim_ixs;
        int *core_offsets;
        char *core_signature;
        PyUFunc_TypeResolutionFunc *type_resolver;
        void *reserved2;
        void *reserved3;
        npy_uint32 *op_flags;
        npy_uint32 *iter_flags;
        /* new in API version 0x0000000D */
        npy_intp *core_dim_sizes;
        npy_uint32 *core_dim_flags;
        PyObject *identity_value;
        /* Further private slots (size depends on the NumPy version) */
    } PyUFuncObject;
    ```
    int nin[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.nin "Link to this definition")
    :   The number of input arguments.
    int nout[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.nout "Link to this definition")
    :   The number of output arguments.
    int nargs[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.nargs "Link to this definition")
    :   The total number of arguments (*nin* + *nout*). This must be
        less than [`NPY_MAXARGS`](array.html#c.NPY_MAXARGS "NPY_MAXARGS").
    int identity[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.identity "Link to this definition")
    :   Either [`PyUFunc_One`](ufunc.html#c.PyUFunc_One "PyUFunc_One"), [`PyUFunc_Zero`](ufunc.html#c.PyUFunc_Zero "PyUFunc_Zero"),
        [`PyUFunc_MinusOne`](ufunc.html#c.PyUFunc_MinusOne "PyUFunc_MinusOne"), [`PyUFunc_None`](ufunc.html#c.PyUFunc_None "PyUFunc_None"),
        [`PyUFunc_ReorderableNone`](ufunc.html#c.PyUFunc_ReorderableNone "PyUFunc_ReorderableNone"), or
        [`PyUFunc_IdentityValue`](ufunc.html#c.PyUFunc_IdentityValue "PyUFunc_IdentityValue") to indicate
        the identity for this operation. It is only used for a
        reduce-like call on an empty array.
    void functions(char \*\*args, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*dims, [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*steps, void \*extradata)[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.functions "Link to this definition")
    :   An array of function pointers — one for each data type
        supported by the ufunc. This is the vector loop that is called
        to implement the underlying function *dims* [0] times. The
        first argument, *args*, is an array of *nargs* pointers to
        behaved memory. Pointers to the data for the input arguments
        are first, followed by the pointers to the data for the output
        arguments. How many bytes must be skipped to get to the next
        element in the sequence is specified by the corresponding entry
        in the *steps* array. The last argument allows the loop to
        receive extra information. This is commonly used so that a
        single, generic vector loop can be used for multiple
        functions. In this case, the actual scalar function to call is
        passed in as *extradata*. The size of this function pointer
        array is ntypes.
    void \*\*data[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.data "Link to this definition")
    :   Extra data to be passed to the 1-d vector loops or `NULL` if
        no extra-data is needed. This C-array must be the same size (
        *i.e.* ntypes) as the functions array. `NULL` is used if
        extra\_data is not needed. Several C-API calls for UFuncs are
        just 1-d vector loops that make use of this extra data to
        receive a pointer to the actual function to call.
    int ntypes[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.ntypes "Link to this definition")
    :   The number of supported data types for the ufunc. This number
        specifies how many different 1-d loops (of the builtin data
        types) are available.
    char \*name[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.name "Link to this definition")
    :   A string name for the ufunc. This is used dynamically to build
        the \_\_doc\_\_ attribute of ufuncs.
    char \*types[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.types "Link to this definition")
    :   An array of \(nargs \times ntypes\) 8-bit type\_numbers
        which contains the type signature for the function for each of
        the supported (builtin) data types. For each of the *ntypes*
        functions, the corresponding set of type numbers in this array
        shows how the *args* argument should be interpreted in the 1-d
        vector loop. These type numbers do not have to be the same type
        and mixed-type ufuncs are supported.
    char \*doc[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.doc "Link to this definition")
    :   Documentation for the ufunc. Should not contain the function
        signature as this is generated dynamically when \_\_doc\_\_ is
        retrieved.
    void \*ptr[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.ptr "Link to this definition")
    :   Any dynamically allocated memory. Currently, this is used for
        dynamic ufuncs created from a python function to store room for
        the types, data, and name members.
    [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*obj[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.obj "Link to this definition")
    :   For ufuncs dynamically created from python functions, this member
        holds a reference to the underlying Python function.
    [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*userloops[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.userloops "Link to this definition")
    :   A dictionary of user-defined 1-d vector loops (stored as CObject
        ptrs) for user-defined types. A loop may be registered by the
        user for any user-defined type. It is retrieved by type number.
        User defined type numbers are always larger than
        [`NPY_USERDEF`](dtype.html#c.NPY_USERDEF "NPY_USERDEF").
    int core\_enabled[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.core_enabled "Link to this definition")
    :   0 for scalar ufuncs; 1 for generalized ufuncs
    int core\_num\_dim\_ix[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.core_num_dim_ix "Link to this definition")
    :   Number of distinct core dimension names in the signature
    int \*core\_num\_dims[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.core_num_dims "Link to this definition")
    :   Number of core dimensions of each argument
    int \*core\_dim\_ixs[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.core_dim_ixs "Link to this definition")
    :   Dimension indices in a flattened form; indices of argument `k` are
        stored in `core_dim_ixs[core_offsets[k] : core_offsets[k] +
        core_numdims[k]]`
    int \*core\_offsets[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.core_offsets "Link to this definition")
    :   Position of 1st core dimension of each argument in `core_dim_ixs`,
        equivalent to cumsum(`core_num_dims`)
    char \*core\_signature[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.core_signature "Link to this definition")
    :   Core signature string
    [PyUFunc\_TypeResolutionFunc](#c.PyUFuncObject.type_resolver.PyUFunc_TypeResolutionFunc "PyUFunc_TypeResolutionFunc") \*type\_resolver[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.type_resolver "Link to this definition")
    :   A function which resolves the types and fills an array with the dtypes
        for the inputs and outputs
        type PyUFunc\_TypeResolutionFunc[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.type_resolver.PyUFunc_TypeResolutionFunc "Link to this definition")
        :   The function pointer type for [`type_resolver`](#c.PyUFuncObject.type_resolver "type_resolver")
    [npy\_uint32](dtype.html#c.npy_uint32 "npy_uint32") op\_flags[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.op_flags "Link to this definition")
    :   Override the default operand flags for each ufunc operand.
    [npy\_uint32](dtype.html#c.npy_uint32 "npy_uint32") iter\_flags[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.iter_flags "Link to this definition")
    :   Override the default nditer flags for the ufunc.
    Added in API version 0x0000000D
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*core\_dim\_sizes[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.core_dim_sizes "Link to this definition")
    :   For each distinct core dimension, the possible
        [frozen](generalized-ufuncs.html#frozen) size if
        [`UFUNC_CORE_DIM_SIZE_INFERRED`](#c.UFUNC_CORE_DIM_SIZE_INFERRED "UFUNC_CORE_DIM_SIZE_INFERRED") is `0`
    [npy\_uint32](dtype.html#c.npy_uint32 "npy_uint32") \*core\_dim\_flags[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.core_dim_flags "Link to this definition")
    :   For each distinct core dimension, a set of flags (
        [`UFUNC_CORE_DIM_CAN_IGNORE`](#c.UFUNC_CORE_DIM_CAN_IGNORE "UFUNC_CORE_DIM_CAN_IGNORE") and
        [`UFUNC_CORE_DIM_SIZE_INFERRED`](#c.UFUNC_CORE_DIM_SIZE_INFERRED "UFUNC_CORE_DIM_SIZE_INFERRED"))
    [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*identity\_value[#](#c.PyUFuncObject.identity_value "Link to this definition")
    :   Identity for reduction, when [`PyUFuncObject.identity`](#c.PyUFuncObject.identity "PyUFuncObject.identity")
        is equal to [`PyUFunc_IdentityValue`](ufunc.html#c.PyUFunc_IdentityValue "PyUFunc_IdentityValue").
UFUNC\_CORE\_DIM\_CAN\_IGNORE[#](#c.UFUNC_CORE_DIM_CAN_IGNORE "Link to this definition")
:   if the dim name ends in `?`
UFUNC\_CORE\_DIM\_SIZE\_INFERRED[#](#c.UFUNC_CORE_DIM_SIZE_INFERRED "Link to this definition")
:   if the dim size will be determined from the operands
    and not from a [frozen](generalized-ufuncs.html#frozen) signature
### PyArrayIter\_Type and PyArrayIterObject[#](#pyarrayiter-type-and-pyarrayiterobject "Link to this heading")
[PyTypeObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyTypeObject "(in Python v3.14)") PyArrayIter\_Type[#](#c.PyArrayIter_Type "Link to this definition")
:   This is an iterator object that makes it easy to loop over an
    N-dimensional array. It is the object returned from the flat
    attribute of an ndarray. It is also used extensively throughout the
    implementation internals to loop over an N-dimensional array. The
    tp\_as\_mapping interface is implemented so that the iterator object
    can be indexed (using 1-d indexing), and a few methods are
    implemented through the tp\_methods table. This object implements the
    next method and can be used anywhere an iterator can be used in
    Python.
type PyArrayIterObject[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject "Link to this definition")
:   The C-structure corresponding to an object of [`PyArrayIter_Type`](#c.PyArrayIter_Type "PyArrayIter_Type") is
    the [`PyArrayIterObject`](#c.PyArrayIterObject "PyArrayIterObject"). The [`PyArrayIterObject`](#c.PyArrayIterObject "PyArrayIterObject") is used to
    keep track of a pointer into an N-dimensional array. It contains associated
    information used to quickly march through the array. The pointer can
    be adjusted in three basic ways: 1) advance to the “next” position in
    the array in a C-style contiguous fashion, 2) advance to an arbitrary
    N-dimensional coordinate in the array, and 3) advance to an arbitrary
    one-dimensional index into the array. The members of the
    [`PyArrayIterObject`](#c.PyArrayIterObject "PyArrayIterObject") structure are used in these
    calculations. Iterator objects keep their own dimension and strides
    information about an array. This can be adjusted as needed for
    “broadcasting,” or to loop over only specific dimensions.
    ```
    typedef struct {
        PyObject_HEAD
        int   nd_m1;
        npy_intp  index;
        npy_intp  size;
        npy_intp  coordinates[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        npy_intp  dims_m1[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        npy_intp  strides[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        npy_intp  backstrides[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        npy_intp  factors[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        PyArrayObject *ao;
        char  *dataptr;
        npy_bool  contiguous;
    } PyArrayIterObject;
    ```
    int nd\_m1[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.nd_m1 "Link to this definition")
    :   \(N-1\) where \(N\) is the number of dimensions in the
        underlying array.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") index[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.index "Link to this definition")
    :   The current 1-d index into the array.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") size[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.size "Link to this definition")
    :   The total size of the underlying array.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*coordinates[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.coordinates "Link to this definition")
    :   An \(N\) -dimensional index into the array.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*dims\_m1[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.dims_m1 "Link to this definition")
    :   The size of the array minus 1 in each dimension.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*strides[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.strides "Link to this definition")
    :   The strides of the array. How many bytes needed to jump to the next
        element in each dimension.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*backstrides[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.backstrides "Link to this definition")
    :   How many bytes needed to jump from the end of a dimension back
        to its beginning. Note that `backstrides[k] == strides[k] *
        dims_m1[k]`, but it is stored here as an optimization.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*factors[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.factors "Link to this definition")
    :   This array is used in computing an N-d index from a 1-d index. It
        contains needed products of the dimensions.
    [PyArrayObject](#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*ao[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.ao "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to the underlying ndarray this iterator was created to
        represent.
    char \*dataptr[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.dataptr "Link to this definition")
    :   This member points to an element in the ndarray indicated by the
        index.
    [npy\_bool](dtype.html#c.npy_bool "npy_bool") contiguous[#](#c.PyArrayIterObject.contiguous "Link to this definition")
    :   This flag is true if the underlying array is
        [`NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS "NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS"). It is used to simplify
        calculations when possible.
How to use an array iterator on a C-level is explained more fully in
later sections. Typically, you do not need to concern yourself with
the internal structure of the iterator object, and merely interact
with it through the use of the macros [`PyArray_ITER_NEXT`](array.html#c.PyArray_ITER_NEXT "PyArray_ITER_NEXT") (it),
[`PyArray_ITER_GOTO`](array.html#c.PyArray_ITER_GOTO "PyArray_ITER_GOTO") (it, dest), or [`PyArray_ITER_GOTO1D`](array.html#c.PyArray_ITER_GOTO1D "PyArray_ITER_GOTO1D")
(it, index). All of these macros require the argument *it* to be a
[PyArrayIterObject](#c.PyArrayIterObject "PyArrayIterObject")\*.
### PyArrayMultiIter\_Type and PyArrayMultiIterObject[#](#pyarraymultiiter-type-and-pyarraymultiiterobject "Link to this heading")
[PyTypeObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyTypeObject "(in Python v3.14)") PyArrayMultiIter\_Type[#](#c.PyArrayMultiIter_Type "Link to this definition")
:   This type provides an iterator that encapsulates the concept of
    broadcasting. It allows \(N\) arrays to be broadcast together
    so that the loop progresses in C-style contiguous fashion over the
    broadcasted array. The corresponding C-structure is the
    [`PyArrayMultiIterObject`](#c.PyArrayMultiIterObject "PyArrayMultiIterObject") whose memory layout must begin any
    object, *obj*, passed in to the [`PyArray_Broadcast`](array.html#c.PyArray_Broadcast "PyArray_Broadcast") (obj)
    function. Broadcasting is performed by adjusting array iterators so
    that each iterator represents the broadcasted shape and size, but
    has its strides adjusted so that the correct element from the array
    is used at each iteration.
type PyArrayMultiIterObject[#](#c.PyArrayMultiIterObject "Link to this definition")
:   ```
    typedef struct {
        PyObject_HEAD
        int numiter;
        npy_intp size;
        npy_intp index;
        int nd;
        npy_intp dimensions[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        PyArrayIterObject *iters[];
    } PyArrayMultiIterObject;
    ```
    int numiter[#](#c.PyArrayMultiIterObject.numiter "Link to this definition")
    :   The number of arrays that need to be broadcast to the same shape.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") size[#](#c.PyArrayMultiIterObject.size "Link to this definition")
    :   The total broadcasted size.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") index[#](#c.PyArrayMultiIterObject.index "Link to this definition")
    :   The current (1-d) index into the broadcasted result.
    int nd[#](#c.PyArrayMultiIterObject.nd "Link to this definition")
    :   The number of dimensions in the broadcasted result.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*dimensions[#](#c.PyArrayMultiIterObject.dimensions "Link to this definition")
    :   The shape of the broadcasted result (only `nd` slots are used).
    [PyArrayIterObject](#c.PyArrayIterObject "PyArrayIterObject") \*\*iters[#](#c.PyArrayMultiIterObject.iters "Link to this definition")
    :   An array of iterator objects that holds the iterators for the
        arrays to be broadcast together. On return, the iterators are
        adjusted for broadcasting.
### PyArrayNeighborhoodIter\_Type and PyArrayNeighborhoodIterObject[#](#pyarrayneighborhooditer-type-and-pyarrayneighborhooditerobject "Link to this heading")
[PyTypeObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyTypeObject "(in Python v3.14)") PyArrayNeighborhoodIter\_Type[#](#c.PyArrayNeighborhoodIter_Type "Link to this definition")
:   This is an iterator object that makes it easy to loop over an
    N-dimensional neighborhood.
type PyArrayNeighborhoodIterObject[#](#c.PyArrayNeighborhoodIterObject "Link to this definition")
:   The C-structure corresponding to an object of
    [`PyArrayNeighborhoodIter_Type`](#c.PyArrayNeighborhoodIter_Type "PyArrayNeighborhoodIter_Type") is the
    [`PyArrayNeighborhoodIterObject`](#c.PyArrayNeighborhoodIterObject "PyArrayNeighborhoodIterObject").
    ```
    typedef struct {
        PyObject_HEAD
        int nd_m1;
        npy_intp index, size;
        npy_intp coordinates[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS]
        npy_intp dims_m1[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        npy_intp strides[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        npy_intp backstrides[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        npy_intp factors[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        PyArrayObject *ao;
        char *dataptr;
        npy_bool contiguous;
        npy_intp bounds[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS][2];
        npy_intp limits[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS][2];
        npy_intp limits_sizes[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        npy_iter_get_dataptr_t translate;
        npy_intp nd;
        npy_intp dimensions[NPY_MAXDIMS_LEGACY_ITERS];
        PyArrayIterObject* _internal_iter;
        char* constant;
        int mode;
    } PyArrayNeighborhoodIterObject;
    ```
### ScalarArrayTypes[#](#scalararraytypes "Link to this heading")
There is a Python type for each of the different built-in data types
that can be present in the array. Most of these are simple wrappers
around the corresponding data type in C. The C-names for these types
are `Py{TYPE}ArrType_Type` where `{TYPE}` can be
> **Bool**, **Byte**, **Short**, **Int**, **Long**, **LongLong**,
> **UByte**, **UShort**, **UInt**, **ULong**, **ULongLong**,
> **Half**, **Float**, **Double**, **LongDouble**, **CFloat**,
> **CDouble**, **CLongDouble**, **String**, **Unicode**, **Void**,
> **Datetime**, **Timedelta**, and **Object**.
These type names are part of the C-API and can therefore be created in
extension C-code. There is also a `PyIntpArrType_Type` and a
`PyUIntpArrType_Type` that are simple substitutes for one of the
integer types that can hold a pointer on the platform. The structure
of these scalar objects is not exposed to C-code. The function
[`PyArray_ScalarAsCtype`](array.html#c.PyArray_ScalarAsCtype "PyArray_ScalarAsCtype") (..) can be used to extract the C-type
value from the array scalar and the function [`PyArray_Scalar`](array.html#c.PyArray_Scalar "PyArray_Scalar")
(…) can be used to construct an array scalar from a C-value.
## Other C-structures[#](#other-c-structures "Link to this heading")
A few new C-structures were found to be useful in the development of
NumPy. These C-structures are used in at least one C-API call and are
therefore documented here. The main reason these structures were
defined is to make it easy to use the Python ParseTuple C-API to
convert from Python objects to a useful C-Object.
### PyArray\_Dims[#](#pyarray-dims "Link to this heading")
type PyArray\_Dims[#](#c.PyArray_Dims "Link to this definition")
:   This structure is very useful when shape and/or strides information
    is supposed to be interpreted. The structure is:
    ```
    typedef struct {
        npy_intp *ptr;
        int len;
    } PyArray_Dims;
    ```
    The members of this structure are
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*ptr[#](#c.PyArray_Dims.ptr "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to a list of ([`npy_intp`](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp")) integers which
        usually represent array shape or array strides.
    int len[#](#c.PyArray_Dims.len "Link to this definition")
    :   The length of the list of integers. It is assumed safe to
        access *ptr* [0] to *ptr* [len-1].
### PyArray\_Chunk[#](#pyarray-chunk "Link to this heading")
type PyArray\_Chunk[#](#c.PyArray_Chunk "Link to this definition")
:   This is equivalent to the buffer object structure in Python up to
    the ptr member. On 32-bit platforms (*i.e.* if [`NPY_SIZEOF_INT`](config.html#c.NPY_SIZEOF_INT "NPY_SIZEOF_INT")
    == [`NPY_SIZEOF_INTP`](config.html#c.NPY_SIZEOF_INTP "NPY_SIZEOF_INTP")), the len member also matches an equivalent
    member of the buffer object. It is useful to represent a generic
    single-segment chunk of memory.
    ```
    typedef struct {
        PyObject_HEAD
        PyObject *base;
        void *ptr;
        npy_intp len;
        int flags;
    } PyArray_Chunk;
    ```
    The members are
    [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*base[#](#c.PyArray_Chunk.base "Link to this definition")
    :   The Python object this chunk of memory comes from. Needed so that
        memory can be accounted for properly.
    void \*ptr[#](#c.PyArray_Chunk.ptr "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer to the start of the single-segment chunk of memory.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") len[#](#c.PyArray_Chunk.len "Link to this definition")
    :   The length of the segment in bytes.
    int flags[#](#c.PyArray_Chunk.flags "Link to this definition")
    :   Any data flags (*e.g.* [`NPY_ARRAY_WRITEABLE`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_WRITEABLE "NPY_ARRAY_WRITEABLE") ) that should
        be used to interpret the memory.
### PyArrayInterface[#](#pyarrayinterface "Link to this heading")
See also
[The array interface protocol](../arrays.interface.html#arrays-interface)
type PyArrayInterface[#](#c.PyArrayInterface "Link to this definition")
:   The [`PyArrayInterface`](#c.PyArrayInterface "PyArrayInterface") structure is defined so that NumPy and
    other extension modules can use the rapid array interface
    protocol. The [`__array_struct__`](../arrays.interface.html#object.__array_struct__ "object.__array_struct__") method of an object that
    supports the rapid array interface protocol should return a
    [`PyCapsule`](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/capsule.html#c.PyCapsule "(in Python v3.14)") that contains a pointer to a [`PyArrayInterface`](#c.PyArrayInterface "PyArrayInterface")
    structure with the relevant details of the array. After the new
    array is created, the attribute should be `DECREF`’d which will
    free the [`PyArrayInterface`](#c.PyArrayInterface "PyArrayInterface") structure. Remember to `INCREF` the
    object (whose [`__array_struct__`](../arrays.interface.html#object.__array_struct__ "object.__array_struct__") attribute was retrieved) and
    point the base member of the new [`PyArrayObject`](#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") to this same
    object. In this way the memory for the array will be managed
    correctly.
    ```
    typedef struct {
        int two;
        int nd;
        char typekind;
        int itemsize;
        int flags;
        npy_intp *shape;
        npy_intp *strides;
        void *data;
        PyObject *descr;
    } PyArrayInterface;
    ```
    int two[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.two "Link to this definition")
    :   the integer 2 as a sanity check.
    int nd[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.nd "Link to this definition")
    :   the number of dimensions in the array.
    char typekind[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.typekind "Link to this definition")
    :   A character indicating what kind of array is present according to the
        typestring convention with ‘t’ -> bitfield, ‘b’ -> Boolean, ‘i’ ->
        signed integer, ‘u’ -> unsigned integer, ‘f’ -> floating point, ‘c’ ->
        complex floating point, ‘O’ -> object, ‘S’ -> (byte-)string, ‘U’ ->
        unicode, ‘V’ -> void.
    int itemsize[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.itemsize "Link to this definition")
    :   The number of bytes each item in the array requires.
    int flags[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.flags "Link to this definition")
    :   Any of the bits [`NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS "NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS") (1),
        [`NPY_ARRAY_F_CONTIGUOUS`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_F_CONTIGUOUS "NPY_ARRAY_F_CONTIGUOUS") (2), [`NPY_ARRAY_ALIGNED`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_ALIGNED "NPY_ARRAY_ALIGNED") (0x100),
        [`NPY_ARRAY_NOTSWAPPED`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_NOTSWAPPED "NPY_ARRAY_NOTSWAPPED") (0x200), or [`NPY_ARRAY_WRITEABLE`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_WRITEABLE "NPY_ARRAY_WRITEABLE")
        (0x400) to indicate something about the data. The
        [`NPY_ARRAY_ALIGNED`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_ALIGNED "NPY_ARRAY_ALIGNED"), [`NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS "NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS"), and
        [`NPY_ARRAY_F_CONTIGUOUS`](array.html#c.NPY_ARRAY_F_CONTIGUOUS "NPY_ARRAY_F_CONTIGUOUS") flags can actually be determined from
        the other parameters. The flag [`NPY_ARR_HAS_DESCR`](../arrays.interface.html#c.NPY_ARR_HAS_DESCR "NPY_ARR_HAS_DESCR")
        (0x800) can also be set to indicate to objects consuming the
        version 3 array interface that the descr member of the
        structure is present (it will be ignored by objects consuming
        version 2 of the array interface).
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*shape[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.shape "Link to this definition")
    :   An array containing the size of the array in each dimension.
    [npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*strides[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.strides "Link to this definition")
    :   An array containing the number of bytes to jump to get to the next
        element in each dimension.
    void \*data[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.data "Link to this definition")
    :   A pointer *to* the first element of the array.
    [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*descr[#](#c.PyArrayInterface.descr "Link to this definition")
    :   A Python object describing the data-type in more detail (same
        as the *descr* key in [`__array_interface__`](../arrays.interface.html#object.__array_interface__ "object.__array_interface__")). This can be
        `NULL` if *typekind* and *itemsize* provide enough
        information. This field is also ignored unless
        [`NPY_ARR_HAS_DESCR`](../arrays.interface.html#c.NPY_ARR_HAS_DESCR "NPY_ARR_HAS_DESCR") flag is on in *flags*.
### Internally used structures[#](#internally-used-structures "Link to this heading")
Internally, the code uses some additional Python objects primarily for
memory management. These types are not accessible directly from
Python, and are not exposed to the C-API. They are included here only
for completeness and assistance in understanding the code.
type PyUFunc\_Loop1d[#](#c.PyUFunc_Loop1d "Link to this definition")
:   A simple linked-list of C-structures containing the information needed
    to define a 1-d loop for a ufunc for every defined signature of a
    user-defined data-type.
[PyTypeObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/type.html#c.PyTypeObject "(in Python v3.14)") PyArrayMapIter\_Type[#](#c.PyArrayMapIter_Type "Link to this definition")
:   Advanced indexing is handled with this Python type. It is simply a
    loose wrapper around the C-structure containing the variables
    needed for advanced array indexing.
type PyArrayMapIterObject[#](#c.PyArrayMapIterObject "Link to this definition")
:   The C-structure associated with [`PyArrayMapIter_Type`](#c.PyArrayMapIter_Type "PyArrayMapIter_Type").
    This structure is useful if you are trying to
    understand the advanced-index mapping code. It is defined in the
    `multiarray/mapping.h` header. This type is not exposed to Python and
    could be replaced with a C-structure. As a Python type it takes
    advantage of reference- counted memory management.
## NumPy C-API and C complex[#](#numpy-c-api-and-c-complex "Link to this heading")
When you use the NumPy C-API, you will have access to complex real declarations
`npy_cdouble` and `npy_cfloat`, which are declared in terms of the C
standard types from `complex.h`. Unfortunately, `complex.h` contains
`#define I ...` (where the actual definition depends on the compiler), which
means that any downstream user that does `#include <numpy/arrayobject.h>`
could get `I` defined, and using something like declaring `double I;` in
their code will result in an obscure compiler error like
This error can be avoided by adding:
```
#undef I
```
to your code.
Changed in version 2.0: The inclusion of `complex.h` was new in NumPy 2, so that code defining
a different `I` may not have required the `#undef I` on older versions.
NumPy 2.0.1 briefly included the `#under I`

* System configuration
# System configuration[#](#system-configuration "Link to this heading")
When NumPy is built, information about system configuration is
recorded, and is made available for extension modules using NumPy’s C
API. These are mostly defined in `numpyconfig.h` (included in
`ndarrayobject.h`). The public symbols are prefixed by `NPY_*`.
NumPy also offers some functions for querying information about the
platform in use.
For private use, NumPy also constructs a `config.h` in the NumPy
include directory, which is not exported by NumPy (that is a python
extension which use the numpy C API will not see those symbols), to
avoid namespace pollution.
## Data type sizes[#](#data-type-sizes "Link to this heading")
The `NPY_SIZEOF_{CTYPE}` constants are defined so that sizeof
information is available to the pre-processor.
NPY\_SIZEOF\_SHORT[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_SHORT "Link to this definition")
:   sizeof(short)
NPY\_SIZEOF\_INT[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_INT "Link to this definition")
:   sizeof(int)
NPY\_SIZEOF\_LONG[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_LONG "Link to this definition")
:   sizeof(long)
NPY\_SIZEOF\_LONGLONG[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_LONGLONG "Link to this definition")
:   sizeof(longlong) where longlong is defined appropriately on the
    platform.
NPY\_SIZEOF\_PY\_LONG\_LONG[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_PY_LONG_LONG "Link to this definition")
NPY\_SIZEOF\_FLOAT[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_FLOAT "Link to this definition")
:   sizeof(float)
NPY\_SIZEOF\_DOUBLE[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_DOUBLE "Link to this definition")
:   sizeof(double)
NPY\_SIZEOF\_LONG\_DOUBLE[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_LONG_DOUBLE "Link to this definition")
NPY\_SIZEOF\_LONGDOUBLE[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_LONGDOUBLE "Link to this definition")
:   sizeof(longdouble)
NPY\_SIZEOF\_PY\_INTPTR\_T[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_PY_INTPTR_T "Link to this definition")
:   Size of a pointer `void *` and `intptr_t`/`Py_intptr_t`.
NPY\_SIZEOF\_INTP[#](#c.NPY_SIZEOF_INTP "Link to this definition")
:   Size of a `size_t` on this platform (`sizeof(size_t)`)
## Platform information[#](#platform-information "Link to this heading")
NPY\_CPU\_X86[#](#c.NPY_CPU_X86 "Link to this definition")
NPY\_CPU\_AMD64[#](#c.NPY_CPU_AMD64 "Link to this definition")
NPY\_CPU\_IA64[#](#c.NPY_CPU_IA64 "Link to this definition")
NPY\_CPU\_PPC[#](#c.NPY_CPU_PPC "Link to this definition")
NPY\_CPU\_PPC64[#](#c.NPY_CPU_PPC64 "Link to this definition")
NPY\_CPU\_SPARC[#](#c.NPY_CPU_SPARC "Link to this definition")
NPY\_CPU\_SPARC64[#](#c.NPY_CPU_SPARC64 "Link to this definition")
NPY\_CPU\_S390[#](#c.NPY_CPU_S390 "Link to this definition")
NPY\_CPU\_PARISC[#](#c.NPY_CPU_PARISC "Link to this definition")
:   CPU architecture of the platform; only one of the above is
    defined.
    Defined in `numpy/npy_cpu.h`
NPY\_LITTLE\_ENDIAN[#](#c.NPY_LITTLE_ENDIAN "Link to this definition")
NPY\_BIG\_ENDIAN[#](#c.NPY_BIG_ENDIAN "Link to this definition")
NPY\_BYTE\_ORDER[#](#c.NPY_BYTE_ORDER "Link to this definition")
:   Portable alternatives to the `endian.h` macros of GNU Libc.
    If big endian, [`NPY_BYTE_ORDER`](#c.NPY_BYTE_ORDER "NPY_BYTE_ORDER") == [`NPY_BIG_ENDIAN`](#c.NPY_BIG_ENDIAN "NPY_BIG_ENDIAN"), and
    similarly for little endian architectures.
    Defined in `numpy/npy_endian.h`.
int PyArray\_GetEndianness()[#](#c.PyArray_GetEndianness "Link to this definition")
:   Returns the endianness of the current platform.
    One of [`NPY_CPU_BIG`](#c.PyArray_GetEndianness.NPY_CPU_BIG "NPY_CPU_BIG"), [`NPY_CPU_LITTLE`](#c.PyArray_GetEndianness.NPY_CPU_LITTLE "NPY_CPU_LITTLE"),
    or [`NPY_CPU_UNKNOWN_ENDIAN`](#c.PyArray_GetEndianness.NPY_CPU_UNKNOWN_ENDIAN "NPY_CPU_UNKNOWN_ENDIAN").
    NPY\_CPU\_BIG[#](#c.PyArray_GetEndianness.NPY_CPU_BIG "Link to this definition")
    NPY\_CPU\_LITTLE[#](#c.PyArray_GetEndianness.NPY_CPU_LITTLE "Link to this definition")
    NPY\_CPU\_UNKNOWN\_ENDIAN[#](#c.PyArray_GetEndianness.NPY_CPU_UNKNOWN_ENDIAN "Link to this definition")
## Compiler directives[#](#compiler-directives "Link to this heading")
NPY\_LIKELY[#](#c.NPY_LIKELY "Link to this definition")
NPY\_UNLIKELY[#](#c.NPY_UNLIKELY "Link to this definition")
NPY\_UNUSED[#](#c.NPY_UNUSED "Link to this definition")

* Data type API
# Data type API[#](#data-type-api "Link to this heading")
The standard array can have 25 different data types (and has some
support for adding your own types). These data types all have an
enumerated type, an enumerated type-character, and a corresponding
array scalar Python type object (placed in a hierarchy). There are
also standard C typedefs to make it easier to manipulate elements of
the given data type. For the numeric types, there are also bit-width
equivalent C typedefs and named typenumbers that make it easier to
select the precision desired.
Warning
The names for the types in c code follows c naming conventions
more closely. The Python names for these types follow Python
conventions. Thus, [`NPY_FLOAT`](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_FLOAT "NPY_FLOAT") picks up a 32-bit float in
C, but [`numpy.float64`](../arrays.scalars.html#numpy.float64 "numpy.float64") in Python corresponds to a 64-bit
double. The bit-width names can be used in both Python and C for
clarity.
## Enumerated types[#](#enumerated-types "Link to this heading")
enum NPY\_TYPES[#](#c.NPY_TYPES "Link to this definition")
:   There is a list of enumerated types defined providing the basic 25
    data types plus some useful generic names. Whenever the code requires
    a type number, one of these enumerated types is requested. The types
    are all called `NPY_{NAME}`:
    enumerator NPY\_BOOL[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_BOOL "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for the boolean type, stored as one byte.
        It may only be set to the values 0 and 1.
    enumerator NPY\_BYTE[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_BYTE "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_INT8[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_INT8 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for an 8-bit/1-byte signed integer.
    enumerator NPY\_SHORT[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_SHORT "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_INT16[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_INT16 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 16-bit/2-byte signed integer.
    enumerator NPY\_INT[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_INT "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_INT32[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_INT32 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 32-bit/4-byte signed integer.
    enumerator NPY\_LONG[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_LONG "Link to this definition")
    :   Equivalent to either NPY\_INT or NPY\_LONGLONG, depending on the
        platform.
    enumerator NPY\_LONGLONG[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_LONGLONG "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_INT64[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_INT64 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 64-bit/8-byte signed integer.
    enumerator NPY\_UBYTE[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UBYTE "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_UINT8[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UINT8 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for an 8-bit/1-byte unsigned integer.
    enumerator NPY\_USHORT[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_USHORT "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_UINT16[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UINT16 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 16-bit/2-byte unsigned integer.
    enumerator NPY\_UINT[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UINT "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_UINT32[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UINT32 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 32-bit/4-byte unsigned integer.
    enumerator NPY\_ULONG[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_ULONG "Link to this definition")
    :   Equivalent to either NPY\_UINT or NPY\_ULONGLONG, depending on the
        platform.
    enumerator NPY\_ULONGLONG[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_ULONGLONG "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_UINT64[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UINT64 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 64-bit/8-byte unsigned integer.
    enumerator NPY\_HALF[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_HALF "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_FLOAT16[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_FLOAT16 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 16-bit/2-byte IEEE 754-2008 compatible floating
        point type.
    enumerator NPY\_FLOAT[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_FLOAT "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_FLOAT32[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_FLOAT32 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 32-bit/4-byte IEEE 754 compatible floating
        point type.
    enumerator NPY\_DOUBLE[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_DOUBLE "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_FLOAT64[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_FLOAT64 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 64-bit/8-byte IEEE 754 compatible floating
        point type.
    enumerator NPY\_LONGDOUBLE[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_LONGDOUBLE "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a platform-specific floating point type which is
        at least as large as NPY\_DOUBLE, but larger on many platforms.
    enumerator NPY\_CFLOAT[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_CFLOAT "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_COMPLEX64[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_COMPLEX64 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 64-bit/8-byte complex type made up of
        two NPY\_FLOAT values.
    enumerator NPY\_CDOUBLE[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_CDOUBLE "Link to this definition")
    enumerator NPY\_COMPLEX128[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_COMPLEX128 "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a 128-bit/16-byte complex type made up of
        two NPY\_DOUBLE values.
    enumerator NPY\_CLONGDOUBLE[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_CLONGDOUBLE "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a platform-specific complex floating point
        type which is made up of two NPY\_LONGDOUBLE values.
    enumerator NPY\_DATETIME[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_DATETIME "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a data type which holds dates or datetimes with
        a precision based on selectable date or time units.
    enumerator NPY\_TIMEDELTA[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_TIMEDELTA "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a data type which holds lengths of times in
        integers of selectable date or time units.
    enumerator NPY\_STRING[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_STRING "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for null-padded byte strings of a selectable
        size. The strings have a fixed maximum size within a given array.
    enumerator NPY\_UNICODE[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UNICODE "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for UCS4 strings of a selectable size. The
        strings have a fixed maximum size within a given array.
    enumerator NPY\_VSTRING[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_VSTRING "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for UTF-8 variable-width strings. Note that this
        dtype holds an array of references, with string data stored outside of
        the array buffer. Use the C API for working with numpy variable-width
        static strings to access the string data in each array entry.
        Note
        This DType is new-style and is not included in `NPY_NTYPES_LEGACY`.
    enumerator NPY\_OBJECT[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_OBJECT "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for references to arbitrary Python objects.
    enumerator NPY\_VOID[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_VOID "Link to this definition")
    :   Primarily used to hold struct dtypes, but can contain arbitrary
        binary data.
    Some useful aliases of the above types are
    enumerator NPY\_INTP[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_INTP "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for a signed integer of type `Py_ssize_t`
        (same as `ssize_t` if defined). This is the type used by all
        arrays of indices.
        Changed in version 2.0: Previously, this was the same as `intptr_t` (same size as a
        pointer). In practice, this is identical except on very niche
        platforms.
        You can use the `'p'` character code for the pointer meaning.
    enumerator NPY\_UINTP[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UINTP "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value for an unsigned integer type that is identical
        to a `size_t`.
        Changed in version 2.0: Previously, this was the same as `uintptr_t` (same size as a
        pointer). In practice, this is identical except on very niche
        platforms.
        You can use the `'P'` character code for the pointer meaning.
    enumerator NPY\_MASK[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_MASK "Link to this definition")
    :   The enumeration value of the type used for masks, such as with
        the [`NPY_ITER_ARRAYMASK`](iterator.html#c.NPY_ITER_ARRAYMASK "NPY_ITER_ARRAYMASK") iterator flag. This is equivalent
        to [`NPY_UINT8`](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_UINT8 "NPY_UINT8").
    enumerator NPY\_DEFAULT\_TYPE[#](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_DEFAULT_TYPE "Link to this definition")
    :   The default type to use when no dtype is explicitly specified, for
        example when calling np.zero(shape). This is equivalent to
        [`NPY_DOUBLE`](#c.NPY_TYPES.NPY_DOUBLE "NPY_DOUBLE").
Other useful related constants are
NPY\_NTYPES\_LEGACY[#](#c.NPY_NTYPES_LEGACY "Link to this definition")
:   The number of built-in NumPy types written using the legacy DType
    system. New NumPy dtypes will be written using the new DType API and may not
    function in the same manner as legacy DTypes. Use this macro if you want to
    handle legacy DTypes using different code paths or if you do not want to
    update code that uses `NPY_NTYPES_LEGACY` and does not work correctly with new
    DTypes.
    Note
    Newly added DTypes such as `NPY_VSTRING` will not be counted
    in `NPY_NTYPES_LEGACY`.
NPY\_NOTYPE[#](#c.NPY_NOTYPE "Link to this definition")
:   A signal value guaranteed not to be a valid type enumeration number.
NPY\_USERDEF[#](#c.NPY_USERDEF "Link to this definition")
:   The start of type numbers used for legacy Custom Data types.
    New-style user DTypes currently are currently *not* assigned a type-number.
    Note
    The total number of user dtypes is limited to below `NPY_VSTRING`.
    Higher numbers are reserved to future new-style DType use.
The various character codes indicating certain types are also part of
an enumerated list. References to type characters (should they be
needed at all) should always use these enumerations. The form of them
is `NPY_{NAME}LTR` where `{NAME}` can be
> **BOOL**, **BYTE**, **UBYTE**, **SHORT**, **USHORT**, **INT**,
> **UINT**, **LONG**, **ULONG**, **LONGLONG**, **ULONGLONG**,
> **HALF**, **FLOAT**, **DOUBLE**, **LONGDOUBLE**, **CFLOAT**,
> **CDOUBLE**, **CLONGDOUBLE**, **DATETIME**, **TIMEDELTA**,
> **OBJECT**, **STRING**, **UNICODE**, **VSTRING**, **VOID**
>
> **INTP**, **UINTP**
>
> **GENBOOL**, **SIGNED**, **UNSIGNED**, **FLOATING**, **COMPLEX**
The latter group of `{NAME}s` corresponds to letters used in the array
interface typestring specification.
## Defines[#](#defines "Link to this heading")
### Max and min values for integers[#](#max-and-min-values-for-integers "Link to this heading")
`NPY_MAX_INT{bits}`, `NPY_MAX_UINT{bits}`, `NPY_MIN_INT{bits}`
:   These are defined for `{bits}` = 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, and 256 and provide
    the maximum (minimum) value of the corresponding (unsigned) integer
    type. Note: the actual integer type may not be available on all
    platforms (i.e. 128-bit and 256-bit integers are rare).
`NPY_MIN_{type}`
:   This is defined for `{type}` = **BYTE**, **SHORT**, **INT**,
    **LONG**, **LONGLONG**, **INTP**
`NPY_MAX_{type}`
:   This is defined for all defined for `{type}` = **BYTE**, **UBYTE**,
    **SHORT**, **USHORT**, **INT**, **UINT**, **LONG**, **ULONG**,
    **LONGLONG**, **ULONGLONG**, **INTP**, **UINTP**
### Number of bits in data types[#](#number-of-bits-in-data-types "Link to this heading")
All `NPY_SIZEOF_{CTYPE}` constants have corresponding
`NPY_BITSOF_{CTYPE}` constants defined. The `NPY_BITSOF_{CTYPE}`
constants provide the number of bits in the data type. Specifically,
the available `{CTYPE}s` are
> **BOOL**, **CHAR**, **SHORT**, **INT**, **LONG**,
> **LONGLONG**, **FLOAT**, **DOUBLE**, **LONGDOUBLE**
### Bit-width references to enumerated typenums[#](#bit-width-references-to-enumerated-typenums "Link to this heading")
All of the numeric data types (integer, floating point, and complex)
have constants that are defined to be a specific enumerated type
number. Exactly which enumerated type a bit-width type refers to is
platform dependent. In particular, the constants available are
`PyArray_{NAME}{BITS}` where `{NAME}` is **INT**, **UINT**,
**FLOAT**, **COMPLEX** and `{BITS}` can be 8, 16, 32, 64, 80, 96, 128,
160, 192, 256, and 512. Obviously not all bit-widths are available on
all platforms for all the kinds of numeric types. Commonly 8-, 16-,
32-, 64-bit integers; 32-, 64-bit floats; and 64-, 128-bit complex
types are available.
### Further integer aliases[#](#further-integer-aliases "Link to this heading")
The constants **NPY\_INTP** and **NPY\_UINTP** refer to an `Py_ssize_t`
and `size_t`.
Although in practice normally true, these types are strictly speaking not
pointer sized and the character codes `'p'` and `'P'` can be used for
pointer sized integers.
(Before NumPy 2, `intp` was pointer size, but this almost never matched
the actual use, which is the reason for the name.)
Since NumPy 2, **NPY\_DEFAULT\_INT** is additionally defined.
The value of the macro is runtime dependent: Since NumPy 2, it maps to
`NPY_INTP` while on earlier versions it maps to `NPY_LONG`.
## C-type names[#](#c-type-names "Link to this heading")
There are standard variable types for each of the numeric data types
and the bool data type. Some of these are already available in the
C-specification. You can create variables in extension code with these
types.
### Boolean[#](#boolean "Link to this heading")
type npy\_bool[#](#c.npy_bool "Link to this definition")
:   unsigned char; The constants [`NPY_FALSE`](array.html#c.NPY_FALSE "NPY_FALSE") and
    [`NPY_TRUE`](array.html#c.NPY_TRUE "NPY_TRUE") are also defined.
### (Un)Signed Integer[#](#un-signed-integer "Link to this heading")
Unsigned versions of the integers can be defined by prepending a ‘u’
to the front of the integer name.
type npy\_byte[#](#c.npy_byte "Link to this definition")
:   char
type npy\_ubyte[#](#c.npy_ubyte "Link to this definition")
:   unsigned char
type npy\_short[#](#c.npy_short "Link to this definition")
:   short
type npy\_ushort[#](#c.npy_ushort "Link to this definition")
:   unsigned short
type npy\_int[#](#c.npy_int "Link to this definition")
:   int
type npy\_uint[#](#c.npy_uint "Link to this definition")
:   unsigned int
type npy\_int16[#](#c.npy_int16 "Link to this definition")
:   16-bit integer
type npy\_uint16[#](#c.npy_uint16 "Link to this definition")
:   16-bit unsigned integer
type npy\_int32[#](#c.npy_int32 "Link to this definition")
:   32-bit integer
type npy\_uint32[#](#c.npy_uint32 "Link to this definition")
:   32-bit unsigned integer
type npy\_int64[#](#c.npy_int64 "Link to this definition")
:   64-bit integer
type npy\_uint64[#](#c.npy_uint64 "Link to this definition")
:   64-bit unsigned integer
type npy\_long[#](#c.npy_long "Link to this definition")
:   long int
type npy\_ulong[#](#c.npy_ulong "Link to this definition")
:   unsigned long int
type npy\_longlong[#](#c.npy_longlong "Link to this definition")
:   long long int
type npy\_ulonglong[#](#c.npy_ulonglong "Link to this definition")
:   unsigned long long int
type npy\_intp[#](#c.npy_intp "Link to this definition")
:   `Py_ssize_t` (a signed integer with the same size as the C `size_t`).
    This is the correct integer for lengths or indexing. In practice this is
    normally the size of a pointer, but this is not guaranteed.
    Note
    Before NumPy 2.0, this was the same as `Py_intptr_t`.
    While a better match, this did not match actual usage in practice.
    On the Python side, we still support `np.dtype('p')` to fetch a dtype
    compatible with storing pointers, while `n` is the correct character
    for the `ssize_t`.
type npy\_uintp[#](#c.npy_uintp "Link to this definition")
:   The C `size_t`/`Py_size_t`.
### (Complex) Floating point[#](#complex-floating-point "Link to this heading")
type npy\_half[#](#c.npy_half "Link to this definition")
:   16-bit float
type npy\_float[#](#c.npy_float "Link to this definition")
:   32-bit float
type npy\_cfloat[#](#c.npy_cfloat "Link to this definition")
:   32-bit complex float
type npy\_double[#](#c.npy_double "Link to this definition")
:   64-bit double
type npy\_cdouble[#](#c.npy_cdouble "Link to this definition")
:   64-bit complex double
type npy\_longdouble[#](#c.npy_longdouble "Link to this definition")
:   long double
type npy\_clongdouble[#](#c.npy_clongdouble "Link to this definition")
:   long complex double
complex types are structures with **.real** and **.imag** members (in
that order).
### Bit-width names[#](#bit-width-names "Link to this heading")
There are also typedefs for signed integers, unsigned integers,
floating point, and complex floating point types of specific bit-
widths. The available type names are
> `npy_int{bits}`, `npy_uint{bits}`, `npy_float{bits}`,
> and `npy_complex{bits}`
where `{bits}` is the number of bits in the type and can be **8**,
**16**, **32**, **64**, 128, and 256 for integer types; 16, **32**
, **64**, 80, 96, 128, and 256 for floating-point types; and 32,
**64**, **128**, 160, 192, and 512 for complex-valued types. Which
bit-widths are available is platform dependent. The bolded bit-widths
are usually available on all platforms.
### Time and timedelta[#](#time-and-timedelta "Link to this heading")
type npy\_datetime[#](#c.npy_datetime "Link to this definition")
:   date or datetime (alias of [`npy_int64`](#c.npy_int64 "npy_int64"))
type npy\_timedelta[#](#c.npy_timedelta "Link to this definition")
:   length of time (alias of [`npy_int64`](#c.npy_int64 "npy_int64"))
## Printf formatting[#](#printf-formatting "Link to this heading")
For help in printing, the following strings are defined as the correct
format specifier in printf and related commands.
NPY\_LONGLONG\_FMT[#](#c.NPY_LONGLONG_FMT "Link to this definition")
NPY\_ULONGLONG\_FMT[#](#c.NPY_ULONGLONG_FMT "Link to this definition")
NPY\_INTP\_FMT[#](#c.NPY_INTP_FMT "Link to this definition")
NPY\_UINTP\_FMT[#](#c.NPY_UINTP_FMT "Link to this definition")
NPY\_LONGDOUBLE\_FMT[#](#c.NPY_LONGDOUBLE_FMT "Link to this definition")

* [NumPy reference](../index.html)

* Array API
# Array API[#](#array-api "Link to this heading")
The test of a first-rate intelligence is the ability to hold two
opposed ideas in the mind at the same time, and still retain the
ability to function.
— *F. Scott Fitzgerald*
For a successful technology, reality must take precedence over public
relations, for Nature cannot be fooled.
— *Richard P. Feynman*
## Array structure and data access[#](#array-structure-and-data-access "Link to this heading")
These macros access the [`PyArrayObject`](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") structure members and are
defined in `ndarraytypes.h`. The input argument, *arr*, can be any
[PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)")\* that is directly interpretable as a
[PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject")\* (any instance of the [`PyArray_Type`](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArray_Type "PyArray_Type")
and its sub-types).
int PyArray\_NDIM([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_NDIM "Link to this definition")
:   The number of dimensions in the array.
int PyArray\_FLAGS([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_FLAGS "Link to this definition")
:   Returns an integer representing the [array-flags](#array-flags).
int PyArray\_TYPE([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_TYPE "Link to this definition")
:   Return the (builtin) typenumber for the elements of this array.
int PyArray\_Pack(const [PyArray\_Descr](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArray_Descr "PyArray_Descr") \*descr, void \*item, const [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*value)[#](#c.PyArray_Pack "Link to this definition")
:   New in version 2.0.
    Sets the memory location `item` of dtype `descr` to `value`.
    The function is equivalent to setting a single array element with a Python
    assignment. Returns 0 on success and -1 with an error set on failure.
    Note
    If the `descr` has the [`NPY_NEEDS_INIT`](types-and-structures.html#c.NPY_NEEDS_INIT "NPY_NEEDS_INIT") flag set, the
    data must be valid or the memory zeroed.
int PyArray\_SETITEM([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr, void \*itemptr, [PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*obj)[#](#c.PyArray_SETITEM "Link to this definition")
:   Convert obj and place it in the ndarray, *arr*, at the place
    pointed to by itemptr. Return -1 if an error occurs or 0 on
    success.
    Note
    In general, prefer the use of [`PyArray_Pack`](#c.PyArray_Pack "PyArray_Pack") when
    handling arbitrary Python objects. Setitem is for example not able
    to handle arbitrary casts between different dtypes.
void PyArray\_ENABLEFLAGS([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr, int flags)[#](#c.PyArray_ENABLEFLAGS "Link to this definition")
:   Enables the specified array flags. This function does no validation,
    and assumes that you know what you’re doing.
void PyArray\_CLEARFLAGS([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr, int flags)[#](#c.PyArray_CLEARFLAGS "Link to this definition")
:   Clears the specified array flags. This function does no validation,
    and assumes that you know what you’re doing.
void \*PyArray\_DATA([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_DATA "Link to this definition")
char \*PyArray\_BYTES([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_BYTES "Link to this definition")
:   These two macros are similar and obtain the pointer to the
    data-buffer for the array. The first macro can (and should be)
    assigned to a particular pointer where the second is for generic
    processing. If you have not guaranteed a contiguous and/or aligned
    array then be sure you understand how to access the data in the
    array to avoid memory and/or alignment problems.
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*PyArray\_DIMS([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_DIMS "Link to this definition")
:   Returns a pointer to the dimensions/shape of the array. The
    number of elements matches the number of dimensions
    of the array. Can return `NULL` for 0-dimensional arrays.
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*PyArray\_SHAPE([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_SHAPE "Link to this definition")
:   A synonym for [`PyArray_DIMS`](#c.PyArray_DIMS "PyArray_DIMS"), named to be consistent with the
    [`shape`](../generated/numpy.ndarray.shape.html#numpy.ndarray.shape "numpy.ndarray.shape") usage within Python.
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") \*PyArray\_STRIDES([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_STRIDES "Link to this definition")
:   Returns a pointer to the strides of the array. The
    number of elements matches the number of dimensions
    of the array.
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") PyArray\_DIM([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr, int n)[#](#c.PyArray_DIM "Link to this definition")
:   Return the shape in the *n* \(^{\textrm{th}}\) dimension.
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") PyArray\_STRIDE([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr, int n)[#](#c.PyArray_STRIDE "Link to this definition")
:   Return the stride in the *n* \(^{\textrm{th}}\) dimension.
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") PyArray\_ITEMSIZE([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_ITEMSIZE "Link to this definition")
:   Return the itemsize for the elements of this array.
    Note that, in the old API that was deprecated in version 1.7, this function
    had the return type `int`.
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") PyArray\_SIZE([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_SIZE "Link to this definition")
:   Returns the total size (in number of elements) of the array.
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") PyArray\_Size([PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*obj)[#](#c.PyArray_Size "Link to this definition")
:   Returns 0 if *obj* is not a sub-class of ndarray. Otherwise,
    returns the total number of elements in the array. Safer version
    of [`PyArray_SIZE`](#c.PyArray_SIZE "PyArray_SIZE") (*obj*).
[npy\_intp](dtype.html#c.npy_intp "npy_intp") PyArray\_NBYTES([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_NBYTES "Link to this definition")
:   Returns the total number of bytes consumed by the array.
[PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*PyArray\_BASE([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_BASE "Link to this definition")
:   This returns the base object of the array. In most cases, this
    means the object which owns the memory the array is pointing at.
    If you are constructing an array using the C API, and specifying
    your own memory, you should use the function [`PyArray_SetBaseObject`](#c.PyArray_SetBaseObject "PyArray_SetBaseObject")
    to set the base to an object which owns the memory.
    If the [`NPY_ARRAY_WRITEBACKIFCOPY`](#c.NPY_ARRAY_WRITEBACKIFCOPY "NPY_ARRAY_WRITEBACKIFCOPY") flag is set, it has a different
    meaning, namely base is the array into which the current array will
    be copied upon copy resolution. This overloading of the base property
    for two functions is likely to change in a future version of NumPy.
[PyArray\_Descr](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArray_Descr "PyArray_Descr") \*PyArray\_DESCR([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_DESCR "Link to this definition")
:   Returns a borrowed reference to the dtype property of the array.
[PyArray\_Descr](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArray_Descr "PyArray_Descr") \*PyArray\_DTYPE([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr)[#](#c.PyArray_DTYPE "Link to this definition")
:   A synonym for PyArray\_DESCR, named to be consistent with the
    ‘dtype’ usage within Python.
[PyObject](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/structures.html#c.PyObject "(in Python v3.14)") \*PyArray\_GETITEM([PyArrayObject](types-and-structures.html#c.PyArrayObject "PyArrayObject") \*arr, void \*itemptr)[#](#c.PyArray_GETITEM "Link to this definition")
:   Get a Python object of a builtin type from the ndarray, *arr*,
    at the location pointed to by itemptr. Return `NULL` on failure.
    [`numpy.ndarray.item`](../generated/numpy.ndarray.item.html#numpy.ndarray.item "numpy.ndarray.item") is identical to PyArray\_GETITEM.
